title: Tabbouleh
subtitle: Lebanese parsley salad with bulgur, tomato, and lemon
excerpt: The authentic Lebanese version: mostly parsley and tomato with a small amount of soaked bulgur wheat, dressed with lemon juice and olive oil. Not a grain salad with parsley, a parsley salad with grain. The ratio is the thing that separates the real dish from the Western interpretation.
sourceNotes: Tabbouleh (also spelled tabouli) is one of the defining salads of Lebanese and wider Levantine cuisine. It appears in Claudia Roden's 'A Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1968) and in Anissa Helou's 'Lebanese Cuisine' (1994). The Lebanese version uses far more parsley than bulgur; the Western version reverses the ratio. The authentic preparation is the reference here.

content.text: The ratio in Lebanese tabbouleh is roughly four parts parsley to one part bulgur. The Western version, sold in supermarkets and served in pubs, reverses this, producing a grain salad in which parsley is a flavouring rather than the main event. Both are called tabbouleh; one of them is tabbouleh.

The bulgur needs no cooking, a 15-minute soak in cold water or the juice of the lemons is enough to soften it. The parsley should be flat-leaf, chopped finely but not to dust. The tomatoes are salted and drained before being added, so they do not water down the dressing. The mint is optional in Lebanon but adds brightness.

items.name: Flat-leaf parsley
items.prepNote: leaves and tender stems only, very finely chopped

items.name: Bulgur wheat
items.prepNote: fine-grade, soaked in cold water for 15 minutes then drained

items.name: Tomato
items.prepNote: ripe, finely diced, salted and drained

items.name: Spring onion
items.prepNote: white and pale green parts, finely sliced

content.text: Soak the bulgur and prepare the tomatoes

Put the {{bulgur-wheat}} in a bowl and cover with cold water. Leave for 15 minutes, then drain in a fine-mesh sieve, pressing out as much water as possible. Meanwhile, salt the diced {{tomato}} generously in a colander and leave to drain for 10 minutes, this draws out excess liquid.

Finely chop the {{parsley-flat}}: it should be very fine, almost minced, but not wet and paste-like. Combine the parsley, drained bulgur, drained tomatoes, {{spring-onion}}, and {{mint}} if using in a bowl. Add the {{lemon}} juice and {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}. Toss well and taste, the dressing should be sharp and bright. Adjust salt and lemon. Rest for 10 minutes before serving for the flavours to settle.

items.fix: Salt the tomatoes and let them drain in a colander for at least 10 minutes. Squeeze the bulgur dry in your fist before adding
items.cause: Tomatoes not salted and drained, or bulgur not squeezed dry

items.fix: Tabbouleh needs aggressive lemon. It should taste sharp and bright, not just oily. Wilted parsley produces a flat, slightly bitter result; use the freshest you can find
items.cause: Not enough lemon or the parsley is not fresh

items.fix: Soak the bulgur in cold water for no more than 15 minutes, then drain well. Chop the parsley fine but stop before it becomes a wet paste
Use a fruity, good-quality extra-virgin olive oil. It is one of the few ingredients and carries significant weight in the finished dish
items.cause: Olive oil is neutral or low quality

content.text: Tabbouleh is Lebanese in origin, its name derived from the Arabic for 'spiced' or 'dipped', and it is the most widely exported dish from Levantine cuisine. The problem with its export is that the grain-to-herb ratio was inverted somewhere in transit, producing what is effectively a bulgur wheat salad. In Lebanon, the parsley is the salad and the bulgur is the garnish. The dish is served as part of a mezze spread, on individual leaves of cos lettuce or with flatbread, and it is eaten immediately rather than kept.
